ethical_notes: Coosa was a Mississippian paramount chiefdom encountered by Hernando de Soto's expedition in 1540 and Tristán de Luna in 1560. The Spanish entradas brought epidemic disease that, together with violent slave-raiding by both Spanish and later English-allied groups, collapsed Coosa from a polity controlling tributary towns across what is now Tennessee, Georgia and Alabama into scattered remnant communities by the early 1600s. The polygon is an approximate paramountcy reach reconstructed from De Soto chronicles and archaeological survey; the chiefdom was a layered network of subordinate towns rather than a state with hard borders. Descendants are part of today's Muscogee (Creek), Coushatta and Cherokee nations. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1500, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Coça
name_variants.context: Spanish chronicles of De Soto and Luna entradas

territory_changes.region: Upper Coosa River valley, modern NW Georgia / SE Tennessee
territory_changes.description: De Soto entrada reaches Coosa; Spanish chroniclers describe a powerful chief receiving tribute from at least seven outlying provinces.
Luna expedition finds Coosa already much diminished from epidemic disease introduced 20 years earlier; archaeological evidence shows abandonment of major mound centers in the late 16th century.
Paramount chiefdom dissolves; surviving population coalesces into Upper Creek towns and Cherokee polities. Demographic loss from disease estimated above 80% across Mississippian Southeast.

sources.citation: Hudson, Charles. Knights of Spain, Warriors of the Sun: Hernando de Soto and the South's Ancient Chiefdoms. University of Georgia Press, 1997
Smith, Marvin T. Coosa: The Rise and Fall of a Southeastern Mississippian Chiefdom. University Press of Florida, 2000

ethical_notes:
Etowah (from Muscogee Etalwa, 'town') was one of the largest Mississippian mound complexes in the southeastern woodlands, with platform mounds, palisades and elite burials containing repoussé copper plates of the Southeastern Ceremonial Complex. The site name 'Etowah' is the colonial English rendering of the Muscogee word; the original polity name is not preserved. Descendant communities are today part of the Muscogee (Creek) Nation, forcibly removed from Georgia along the Trail of Tears in the 1830s. The polygon approximates the chiefdom's likely tributary catchment rather than a fixed border. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1279, strategy=fuzzy_name, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

[v6.30-displaced-rollback] Boundary reverted to capital-based approximation: aourednik fuzzy match placed polygon centroid=11567km / edge=10780km from capital (exceeded thresholds). Regenerated as 100.0km radius circle.

name_variants.name: Etowah
name_variants.context: English transliteration used at the archaeological site

territory_changes.description: Etowah enters its peak Wilbanks phase, building Mound A to ~19 m height and dominating tributary towns along the Etowah and upper Coosa rivers.
Site is largely abandoned, possibly after a violent attack — palisade burned, elite mortuary mound looted in antiquity. Cause debated (warfare, climate, factional collapse).
De Soto expedition passes through Itaba (likely Etowah), a partly reoccupied site; subsequent Spanish-introduced epidemics decimate residual Mississippian populations.
Descendant Muscogee and Cherokee communities forcibly removed by US Army on the Trail of Tears; thousands die during deportation to Indian Territory.

sources.citation: King, Adam. Etowah: The Political History of a Chiefdom Capital. University of Alabama Press, 2003
Pauketat, Timothy R. Cahokia: Ancient America's Great City on the Mississippi. Penguin, 2010

ethical_notes: The Mississippian center on the Black Warrior River known today as Moundville was, at its 13th–14th century apogee, one of the largest pre-contact settlements north of Mexico, with 29 platform mounds enclosed by a bastioned palisade. The indigenous name of the polity is unrecorded; 'Moundville' is the modern archaeological label. Descendant communities are most plausibly ancestors of the Choctaw and other Muskogean peoples, forcibly removed under the 1830 Indian Removal Act. The polygon approximates the polity's tributary reach along the Black Warrior valley. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1279, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Black Warrior chiefdom
name_variants.context: Geographic label used in archaeological literature

territory_changes.region: Black Warrior River valley, west-central Alabama
territory_changes.description: Moundville reaches peak as a regional paramountcy with thousands of residents, satellite mound centers and far-flung exchange networks for marine shell, copper and galena.
Population disperses into smaller communities; mound construction ceases. Site continues as a sacred necropolis but the political center fragments before European contact.
De Soto entrada crosses the region (likely province of Apafalaya); subsequent epidemic waves devastate descendant populations.

sources.citation: Knight, Vernon J., and Vincas P. Steponaitis (eds.). Archaeology of the Moundville Chiefdom. Smithsonian Institution Press, 1998
Blitz, John H. Moundville. University of Alabama Press, 2008

ethical_notes: The Calusa were a non-agricultural maritime kingdom of southwest Florida whose paramount ruler at Calos (Mound Key) extracted tribute from a network of subordinate towns across the Caloosahatchee, Ten Thousand Islands and Lake Okeechobee region. They built monumental shell-mound architecture including canals and an artificial lagoon. Spanish missionaries and slave-raiding by English-armed Yamasee and Creek allies in the early 18th century caused near-total demographic collapse: by the 1763 transfer of Florida to Britain, the surviving Calusa were evacuated to Cuba, ending the polity. The few descendants likely merged with Cuban populations or joined the emerging Seminole. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1279, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Calos
name_variants.context: Spanish-period spelling, also the name of the capital

name_variants.name: Carlos
name_variants.context: Hispanicized form of the dynastic title

territory_changes.description: Juan Ponce de León is repulsed by Calusa warriors near Estero Bay; he dies in 1521 of wounds inflicted on a second expedition.
Pedro Menéndez de Avilés founds Fort San Antón de Carlos at the Calusa capital; Spanish Jesuit mission fails by 1569 amid mutual hostility.
Decades of slave raids by English-allied Yamasee, Creek and Uchise reduce the Calusa from estimated 4,000–10,000 to a few hundred refugees concentrated near Spanish Key West.
Spain cedes Florida to Britain; the last estimated 80 surviving Calusa families are evacuated to Cuba. The polity ends; no organized successor community exists today.

sources.citation: Marquardt, William H., and Karen J. Walker (eds.). The Archaeology of Pineland: A Coastal Southwest Florida Site Complex, A.D. 50–1710. Institute of Archaeology and Paleoenvironmental Studies, University of Florida, 2013
Hann, John H. Indians of Central and South Florida, 1513–1763. University Press of Florida, 2003

ethical_notes: The Apalachee were a Muskogean agricultural chiefdom of the Florida panhandle whose capital Anhaica (modern Tallahassee) hosted De Soto's army in winter 1539–40. After Franciscan mission incorporation in the 17th century, the population was annihilated in 1704 by English Carolinian raids led by James Moore and Creek allies: thousands were enslaved and shipped to Carolina, missions were burned, and the survivors fled to Mobile under French protection. The Apalachee polity ended with this raid; a small descendant community (the Talimali Band) maintains identity in Louisiana today. Population loss exceeded 95% across the 17th century. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1400, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Anhaica
name_variants.context: Capital town name, used metonymically for the polity

territory_changes.description: Pánfilo de Narváez expedition reaches Apalachee; survivors (including Cabeza de Vaca) flee after Apalachee resistance.
Franciscan missions established; Apalachee incorporated into the Spanish Florida mission province with a system of forced labor (repartimiento).

Carolinian colonel James Moore and approximately 1,000 Creek allies destroy the Apalachee missions; an estimated 1,000–2,000 Apalachee killed, and 4,000–5,000 enslaved and forcibly marched to Carolina to be sold into Caribbean and North American plantation slavery.

sources.citation: Hann, John H. Apalachee: The Land Between the Rivers. University Presses of Florida, 1988
Gallay, Alan. The Indian Slave Trade: The Rise of the English Empire in the American South, 1670–1717. Yale University Press, 2002

ethical_notes: The Three Fires Confederacy (Niswi-mishkodewinan) is the alliance of the three Anishinaabe nations — Ojibwe (Keepers of the Faith), Odawa (Keepers of the Trade) and Bodéwadmi/Potawatomi (Keepers of the Fire) — which controlled the upper Great Lakes from before European contact through the 19th century. Anishinaabe oral history records this as the Western migration's culmination at Michilimackinac. The polygon is an approximation of the confederacy's traditional reach, not a state border; sovereignty here was kinship- and clan-based. US and Canadian governments fragmented this territory through coerced treaties (Greenville 1795, Saginaw 1819, Washington 1836) and the Indian Removal Act displaced many Potawatomi to Kansas (the 'Trail of Death' 1838). Today the three nations remain sovereign nations on both sides of the colonial border.

territory_changes.region: Upper Great Lakes (Michilimackinac, Sault Ste. Marie)
territory_changes.description: Confederacy traditionally formed at the Straits of Mackinac after the Anishinaabe Western migration from the Atlantic coast.
Treaty of Montreal (Great Peace of 1701) ends the Beaver Wars; Three Fires nations consolidate dominance over former Iroquois-contested western Great Lakes.

Pontiac (Odawa) leads pan-Indian war against British occupation of former French forts; the war fails to dislodge the British but forces the Royal Proclamation recognizing Indigenous land rights west of the Appalachians.
US Army forcibly relocates the Potawatomi to Kansas on the 'Trail of Death'; ~40 die en route, ~660 reach Osawatomie.

sources.citation: Witgen, Michael. An Infinity of Nations: How the Native New World Shaped Early North America. University of Pennsylvania Press, 2012
Benton-Banai, Edward. The Mishomis Book: The Voice of the Ojibway. University of Minnesota Press, 2010

ethical_notes: Lenapehoking ('the Land of the Lenape') was the homeland of the Lenape (Delaware) people — comprising the Munsee in the north (lower Hudson, upper Delaware) and the Unami in the south (Delaware River basin to the Atlantic) — who lived as autonomous matrilineal village communities loosely linked through clan ties. Dutch and Swedish colonization (1620s–1650s) was followed by the fraudulent 1737 'Walking Purchase' under Pennsylvania, by which the Penn family used a rigged 'day-and-a-half walk' to seize 1.2 million acres. Lenape were progressively displaced westward through Ohio, Indiana and Kansas to present-day Oklahoma and Ontario. The federally and provincially recognized Delaware nations today are the legal successors. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1400, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Delaware
name_variants.context: English colonial designation, from the river named for Lord De La Warr

territory_changes.description: Henry Hudson's voyage initiates regular Dutch contact; subsequent epidemic waves (smallpox 1633, 1645, 1659) reduce Lenape population by an estimated 80–90%.
Walking Purchase: Pennsylvania officials fraudulently extend a treaty 'day-and-a-half walk' using trained runners and pre-cleared paths to seize ~4,860 km² of Lenape land.
Pennsylvania militia under David Williamson murder 96 unarmed Christian Lenape (28 men, 29 women, 39 children) at Gnadenhütten mission, scalping the bodies for bounties.
Final forced removal of the Delaware Tribe of Indians from Kansas to the Cherokee Nation in Oklahoma.

sources.citation: Weslager, C. A. The Delaware Indians: A History. Rutgers University Press, 1972
Schutt, Amy C. Peoples of the River Valleys: The Odyssey of the Delaware Indians. University of Pennsylvania Press, 2007

ethical_notes: The Shawnee (Shawanwaki — 'Southerners' in their own Algonquian language) were organized into five matrilineal divisions (Chalakaatha, Hathawekela, Kispoko, Mekoche, Pekowi), each providing distinct civil or war leadership. They occupied a vast and shifting territory across the Ohio Valley and were repeatedly dispersed and reassembled by colonial pressures: Iroquois Beaver Wars (1660s), Anglo-French frontier wars, and especially the US conquest of the Ohio Country culminating in Tecumseh's death at the Thames (1813), the destruction of the spiritual capital Prophetstown (1811), and removal under the 1830 Indian Removal Act to Kansas and ultimately Oklahoma. Three federally recognized Shawnee nations exist today; the homeland in Ohio remains unceded by descendant communities.

territory_changes.description: Iroquois Beaver Wars displace Shawnee from Ohio Valley; bands scatter to Pennsylvania, the Carolinas, Maryland, and the Illinois Country.
Shawnee bands reassemble in the Ohio Country, founding towns (Lower Shawneetown, Chillicothe variants).
US forces under William Henry Harrison destroy Prophetstown, the spiritual capital led by Tenskwatawa, while Tecumseh is south recruiting allies.
Shawnee in Ohio forced to cede the last reservations and remove west of the Mississippi under the Indian Removal Act.

sources.citation: Calloway, Colin G. The Shawnees and the War for America. Penguin, 2008
Sugden, John. Tecumseh: A Life. Henry Holt, 1998

ethical_notes: The Chikasha (Chickasaw) controlled the headwaters of the Tombigbee and Yazoo rivers in present-day northern Mississippi and western Tennessee, defeating successive French invasions (1736, 1739, 1740) that sought to break their English-allied trade. As one of the so-called 'Five Civilized Tribes,' they were nonetheless forcibly removed under the 1832 Treaty of Pontotoc Creek and the 1837 Treaty of Doaksville: an estimated 4,000–5,000 Chikasha were marched to Indian Territory; hundreds died en route. The Chickasaw Nation reorganized in Oklahoma and is today a sovereign federally recognized nation. The polygon approximates the homeland; the legal successor is in Oklahoma.

territory_changes.description: De Soto winters in Chikasha territory; following a dispute, Chikasha night attack drives the Spanish out with heavy losses.
Chikasha decisively defeat French and Choctaw invasion at the Battles of Ackia and Ogoula Tchetoka.
Chickasaw Removal: ~4,000–5,000 Chikasha forced to march to Indian Territory under US Army supervision; significant deaths from exposure, disease and dysentery.

sources.citation: Atkinson, James R. Splendid Land, Splendid People: The Chickasaw Indians to Removal. University of Alabama Press, 2004
Saunt, Claudio. Unworthy Republic: The Dispossession of Native Americans and the Road to Indian Territory. W. W. Norton, 2020

ethical_notes: The Chahta (Choctaw) coalesced as a confederation of three regional 'iksa' (divisions) in the central and southern Mississippi Valley after the Mississippian collapse. Their sacred mound Nanih Waiya is the origin point in Choctaw cosmology. Under the 1830 Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek — the first removal treaty under the Indian Removal Act, signed under coercion — between 14,000 and 17,000 Chahta were forcibly displaced to Indian Territory in 1831–1833; an estimated 2,500 died of cholera, exposure and starvation along what the Choctaw call the 'Trail of Tears and Death.' Three federally recognized Choctaw nations exist today (Oklahoma, Mississippi Band, Jena Band).

territory_changes.description: Choctaw align with French Louisiana, becoming the principal Indigenous military power in the lower Mississippi against English-allied Chickasaw.
Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek: under threat and bribery, Chahta leaders cede ~44,000 km² of homeland east of the Mississippi.
First Choctaw removal: ~14,000–17,000 forced west; ~2,500 die en route from cholera and exposure during winter marches.

sources.citation: Galloway, Patricia. Choctaw Genesis, 1500–1700. University of Nebraska Press, 1995
Akers, Donna L. Removing the Heart of the Choctaw People: Indian Removal from a Native Perspective. American Indian Culture and Research Journal 23(3), 1999

ethical_notes: The Pawnee (Chaticks-si-Chaticks, 'Men of Men') were a Caddoan-speaking confederation of four bands (Chaui, Kitkehahki, Pitahawirata, Skidi) of earth-lodge agriculturalists and bison hunters along the Platte and Republican rivers in present-day Nebraska. Smallpox waves in 1798, 1831 and especially 1837–38 reduced the population from an estimated 12,000 to under 4,000; combined with Lakota raiding, US encroachment, and forced cession of homeland (1833, 1857, 1875), the Pawnee were removed to Indian Territory in 1875. The Pawnee Nation in Oklahoma is today a sovereign federally recognized nation. Some Pawnee men served as US Army scouts, a fact that underscores the impossible choices forced by US conquest.

territory_changes.description: 1837–38 smallpox epidemic kills an estimated half of the Pawnee population.
Treaty of Table Creek: Pawnee cede most remaining land, retain a small reservation on the Loup Fork.
Lakota war party kills ~70 Pawnee on a sanctioned bison hunt; the loss accelerates Pawnee acceptance of removal.
Forced removal of the surviving Pawnee population to Indian Territory; further population loss to disease en route and at the new reservation.

sources.citation: Hyde, George E. The Pawnee Indians. University of Oklahoma Press, 1988 (rev. ed.)
Wishart, David J. An Unspeakable Sadness: The Dispossession of the Nebraska Indians. University of Nebraska Press, 1994

ethical_notes: The Nueta (Mandan, 'people of the riverbank') were Siouan-speaking earth-lodge agriculturalists of the Upper Missouri who were the primary trading hub between Plains nomads, Cree-Assiniboine middlemen, and later European traders. The 1837 smallpox epidemic — introduced by an American Fur Company steamboat and worsened by the company's failure to vaccinate or quarantine — killed an estimated 90% of the Mandan, reducing the population from ~1,600 to roughly 125 survivors and effectively ending the Nueta as an independent polity. Survivors merged with the Hidatsa and Arikara as the Three Affiliated Tribes (MHA Nation) on the Fort Berthold Reservation in present-day North Dakota.

name_variants.name: Mandan
name_variants.context: English transliteration; etymology disputed (possibly from Sioux 'Mawatani')

territory_changes.description: First documented smallpox epidemic kills ~75% of the Mandan; villages consolidate northward toward the Knife River.
Lewis and Clark expedition winters at Fort Mandan, recording Nueta society in detail.

Smallpox introduced by the steamboat St. Peter's kills ~90% of the Mandan, reducing the nation from ~1,600 to ~125 survivors. American Fur Company is documented as having failed to vaccinate or quarantine the infected crew.
Mandan survivors join Hidatsa and (later) Arikara at Like-a-Fishhook Village, beginning the Three Affiliated Tribes.

sources.citation: Wood, W. Raymond, and Thomas D. Thiessen (eds.). Early Fur Trade on the Northern Plains: Canadian Traders Among the Mandan and Hidatsa Indians, 1738–1818. University of Oklahoma Press, 1985
Fenn, Elizabeth A. Encounters at the Heart of the World: A History of the Mandan People. Hill and Wang, 2014

ethical_notes: The Hiraacá (Hidatsa) were Siouan-speaking earth-lodge horticulturalists and bison hunters along the Knife River and middle Missouri, organized in three village divisions (Awatixa, Awaxawi, Hidatsa-proper). They are the maternal kin of Sakakawea (Sacagawea), born Shoshone but adopted into a Hidatsa village. The 1837 smallpox epidemic killed roughly half the Hiraacá; survivors joined the Mandan at Like-a-Fishhook Village (1845), and were later forcibly relocated by the federal government when the Garrison Dam (completed 1953) flooded most of the Fort Berthold Reservation, displacing 80% of MHA tribal members from their bottomland homes — a violation of the 1851 Treaty of Fort Laramie still unresolved.

name_variants.name: Gros Ventres of the Missouri
name_variants.context: French/English misnomer (distinct from the Atsina 'Gros Ventres of the Prairie')

territory_changes.description: Lewis and Clark winter near the Hidatsa villages; Sakakawea and her husband Toussaint Charbonneau are recruited from Awatixa Village.
Smallpox epidemic kills approximately 50% of the Hidatsa.
Treaty of Fort Laramie: Hidatsa, Mandan and Arikara recognized homeland of ~31,000 km².
Garrison Dam floods 156,000 acres of MHA bottomland; ~80% of tribal members are forced to relocate to upland areas, losing the agricultural base of the reservation.

sources.citation: Bowers, Alfred W. Hidatsa Social and Ceremonial Organization. Smithsonian Institution, 1965 (repr. University of Nebraska Press, 1992)
VanDevelder, Paul. Coyote Warrior: One Man, Three Tribes, and the Trial That Forged a Nation. Little, Brown, 2004

ethical_notes: The Sahnish (Arikara) were Caddoan-speaking earth-lodge agriculturalists who migrated up the Missouri from the central Plains, originally part of the same linguistic family as the Pawnee. Successive smallpox epidemics from the 1780s onward — and the 1823 Arikara War, in which the US Army's first westward military campaign against an Indigenous nation under Henry Leavenworth burned villages — reduced the Sahnish from an estimated 30,000+ in 1700 to fewer than 1,000 by mid-19th century. They joined the Mandan and Hidatsa at Like-a-Fishhook Village (1862) and form the Three Affiliated Tribes (MHA Nation) on the Fort Berthold Reservation today, with the same Garrison Dam dispossession in 1953.

territory_changes.description: Smallpox waves of the 1780s kill an estimated two-thirds of the Sahnish, collapsing their dozens of villages into a handful.
Arikara War: US Army Colonel Henry Leavenworth attacks Sahnish villages with 220 troops and Sioux allies; villages bombarded, crops burned. Survivors flee north.
1837 smallpox epidemic further reduces the surviving Sahnish.
Sahnish join the Mandan and Hidatsa at Like-a-Fishhook, completing the formation of what would become the MHA Nation.

sources.citation: Parks, Douglas R. Traditional Narratives of the Arikara Indians. University of Nebraska Press, 1991
Meyer, Roy W. The Village Indians of the Upper Missouri: The Mandans, Hidatsas, and Arikaras. University of Nebraska Press, 1977

ethical_notes: The Beothuk were the indigenous people of Newfoundland (Ktaqmkuk in neighboring Mi'kmaq), distinct from any other recorded language family. Their use of red ochre on skin and possessions gave rise to the European term 'Red Indian.' Following first contact with European fishermen around 1500, the Beothuk avoided trade and were progressively cut off from coastal resources by Mi'kmaq encroachment and English settlement. They suffered murder by settlers (with bounties paid in some periods), starvation, and tuberculosis. The last known Beothuk, Shanawdithit, died of tuberculosis in St. John's in 1829, ending the people as an organized nation. This is one of the documented cases of complete extinction by colonization in North America. Some descendant DNA persists in Mi'kmaq communities through inter-marriage.

name_variants.name: Red Indians
name_variants.context: Pejorative English colonial designation, originally referring to Beothuk red-ochre body paint

territory_changes.description: European fishermen begin seasonal use of Newfoundland coast; Beothuk withdraw from coastal sites in response to violence and resource competition.
British Governor Hugh Palliser issues a proclamation outlawing violence against Beothuk, but enforcement is essentially nil; settler-Beothuk killings continue.
John Peyton Jr. and a settler party kill several Beothuk and capture Demasduit (Mary March). She dies of tuberculosis in 1820 before being returned to her people.

Death of Shanawdithit, the last documented Beothuk, from tuberculosis. The Beothuk as a distinct nation cease to exist. This colonial-era extinction was the result of dispossession, settler violence, starvation and introduced disease.

sources.citation: Marshall, Ingeborg. A History and Ethnography of the Beothuk. McGill-Queen's University Press, 1996
Pastore, Ralph T. Shanawdithit's People: The Archaeology of the Beothuks. Atlantic Archaeology Ltd., 1992

ethical_notes: Haak'u (Acoma, 'place that always was') is one of the oldest continuously inhabited communities in North America, atop a 110-meter sandstone mesa in west-central New Mexico. In January 1599, after the Acoma killed a Spanish raiding party led by Juan de Zaldívar, governor Juan de Oñate retaliated with what became the Acoma Massacre: the village was stormed, an estimated 800–1,000 Acoma killed, ~500 enslaved, and the Spanish court sentenced surviving men over 25 to have one foot amputated and 20 years of slavery. This event is recognized in modern scholarship as a foundational atrocity of Spanish colonization in New Mexico; in 1998 the Acoma asked New Mexico to remove the foot from a statue of Oñate, an act later carried out symbolically. Acoma Pueblo remains a sovereign federally recognized pueblo today.

name_variants.name: Sky City
name_variants.context: English descriptive name for the mesa-top village

territory_changes.description: Coronado expedition's captain Hernando de Alvarado is the first European to visit Acoma.
Acoma's caciques perform an act of obedience to Oñate; tensions rise over Spanish demands for tribute (cornmeal, textiles, water).

Acoma Massacre: Spanish forces under Vicente de Zaldívar storm Haak'u with cannon and arquebus, killing an estimated 800–1,000 Acoma. Approximately 500 survivors are enslaved; the court sentences male captives over 25 to amputation of one foot and 20 years of personal servitude. Children under 12 are taken to be raised as servants in Spanish households.
Acoma joins the Pueblo Revolt led by Po'pay; Spanish driven out of New Mexico for 12 years.

sources.citation: Knaut, Andrew L. The Pueblo Revolt of 1680: Conquest and Resistance in Seventeenth-Century New Mexico. University of Oklahoma Press, 1995
Sando, Joe S. Pueblo Nations: Eight Centuries of Pueblo Indian History. Clear Light Publishers, 1992

ethical_notes: The A:shiwi (Zuni) consolidated in the late 13th century into a cluster of six villages — Halona:wa, Hawikuh, Kwa'kin'a, Mats'a:kya, Kya:k'ima, Kechiba:wa — collectively the 'Cities of Cíbola' that drew Spanish expeditions seeking the mythical golden cities. In 1540 Coronado's army stormed and occupied Hawikuh; the A:shiwi resisted Spanish missionization throughout the 17th century, killed the Franciscans Letrado and Arvide (1632), and joined the 1680 Pueblo Revolt that expelled Spain for 12 years. After Spanish reconquest (1692), the A:shiwi consolidated into a single village at Halona:wa, the modern Zuni Pueblo. The pueblo is today a sovereign federally recognized nation; its language is a linguistic isolate. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1492, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Cíbola
name_variants.context: 16th-century Spanish name for the A:shiwi region; source of the 'Seven Cities of Gold' myth

territory_changes.description: Estevanico (an enslaved Moroccan guide for fray Marcos de Niza) reaches Hawikuh and is killed by A:shiwi guards; Marcos returns claiming sight of golden cities.
Coronado's army storms and occupies Hawikuh, killing several A:shiwi defenders and seizing food stores.
A:shiwi join the Pueblo Revolt; Spanish missionaries and settlers killed or expelled; A:shiwi withdraw to defensive position at Dowa Yalanne (Corn Mountain).
After Spanish reconquest under Diego de Vargas, the A:shiwi consolidate into a single village at Halona:wa Idiwan'a.

sources.citation: Ferguson, T. J., and Richard E. Hart. A Zuni Atlas. University of Oklahoma Press, 1985
Riley, Carroll L. Becoming Aztlan: Mesoamerican Influence in the Greater Southwest, AD 1200–1500. University of Utah Press, 2005

ethical_notes: Hopituh Shi-nu-mu ('the peaceful little ones') are the people of the Hopi mesas in northeastern Arizona, descendants of the Ancestral Puebloans. Old Oraibi (founded c. 1100 CE) is among the oldest continuously inhabited settlements in North America. The Hopi joined the 1680 Pueblo Revolt and uniquely never re-accepted Spanish missions: in 1700, Hopi from the other mesas destroyed the village of Awatovi, which had readmitted Franciscan missionaries, killing many of its men. Hopi sovereignty is now compromised by the federally imposed Hopi-Navajo land partition (1974 Joint Use Area dispute and 1996 settlement) which forced relocation of thousands of Diné families from contested lands but also constrained Hopi claims. The Hopi Tribe is a federally recognized nation today.

name_variants.name: Moqui
name_variants.context: Spanish/early American name, now considered pejorative

territory_changes.description: Pedro de Tovar (Coronado expedition) reaches the Hopi mesas; brief skirmish at Awatovi.
Franciscans establish missions at Awatovi, Shongopovi and Oraibi over Hopi resistance.
Hopi join the Pueblo Revolt: Franciscan missionaries killed and missions destroyed across all Hopi villages.

Hopi from Walpi, Mishongnovi and Oraibi destroy the village of Awatovi, which had readmitted Franciscan missionaries; men of the village killed, women and children dispersed to other Hopi villages. Awatovi is permanently abandoned.

sources.citation: Brooks, James F. Mesa of Sorrows: A History of the Awatovi Massacre. W. W. Norton, 2016
Whiteley, Peter M. Deliberate Acts: Changing Hopi Culture Through the Oraibi Split. University of Arizona Press, 1988

ethical_notes: The Tongva (also Kizh) inhabited the Los Angeles Basin and the southern Channel Islands (Pimu/Catalina) for at least 1,500 years prior to Spanish colonization, organized in autonomous villages linked by trade in soapstone, shell beads and plank canoes. Following the establishment of Mission San Gabriel Arcángel (1771) — built on the village of Sibapet — the Tongva were forcibly congregated into mission compounds, where coerced labor, sexual violence, and introduced disease reduced the population from an estimated 5,000–10,000 in 1769 to under 1,000 by 1834 secularization. The state of California paid bounties for Indigenous scalps in the early 1850s. The Tongva are not federally recognized as of 2026, though they remain organized as the Gabrielino-Tongva Tribe and have ongoing recognition petitions.

name_variants.name: Gabrieleño
name_variants.context: Spanish mission-era name, after Mission San Gabriel

name_variants.name: Kizh
name_variants.context: Alternative self-designation used by some bands

territory_changes.description: Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo's expedition is the first documented European contact with Tongva, who paddle plank canoes (ti'at) out to meet his ships.
Mission San Gabriel Arcángel founded on Tongva land; over the following decades, most Tongva are forcibly congregated into the mission, where infant mortality reaches above 75% in some years.
Mission registers and demographic studies indicate Tongva population reduced by ~80% from pre-contact baseline due to disease, malnutrition and forced labor.

California state legislature appropriates $1.1 million for 'suppression of Indian hostilities,' funding militia campaigns and scalp bounties; an estimated 80% of California's Indigenous population is killed or starved between 1846 and 1873 statewide.

sources.citation: McCawley, William. The First Angelinos: The Gabrielino Indians of Los Angeles. Malki Museum / Ballena Press, 1996
Madley, Benjamin. An American Genocide: The United States and the California Indian Catastrophe, 1846–1873. Yale University Press, 2016

ethical_notes: Ñuu Tnoo Huahi Andehui ('Black Town, Temple of Heaven') was the most prestigious Mixtec dynastic seat of the Postclassic era, ruled in the 11th century by the conquering king 8 Deer Jaguar Claw (Iya Nacuaa Teyusi Ñaña), whose campaigns are recorded in the Codex Zouche-Nuttall, Codex Bodley and Codex Colombino — sophisticated screen-fold pictographic histories that constitute one of the few surviving pre-Columbian indigenous historical archives. Mixtec kingdoms paid tribute to the Aztec Empire from c. 1458 and were conquered by Pedro de Alvarado for Spain in 1521–22. The codex tradition was systematically destroyed by Spanish friars who burned indigenous books as 'works of the devil'; the surviving handful preserve Ñuu Savi (Mixtec) historical memory only fragmentarily. Speakers of Mixtec (Tu'un Savi) number ~500,000 today. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1200, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Tilantongo
name_variants.context: Nahuatl name 'Tlilantongo' adopted by the Spanish; means 'Black Town'

territory_changes.description: 8 Deer Jaguar Claw is enthroned at Tilantongo and conducts a campaign of conquest across the Mixteca recorded in Codex Zouche-Nuttall.
Aztec emperor Moctezuma I conquers the Mixteca, imposing tribute in jade, gold, feathers and warrior service.
Pedro de Alvarado conquers the Mixtec kingdoms for Spain after the fall of Tenochtitlan; Tilantongo is reduced to a colonial cabecera.

sources.citation: Pohl, John M. D. The Politics of Symbolism in the Mixtec Codices. Vanderbilt University Press, 1994
Jansen, Maarten, and Gabina Aurora Pérez Jiménez. Encounter with the Plumed Serpent: Drama and Power in the Heart of Mesoamerica. University Press of Colorado, 2007

ethical_notes: Cholōllān (Cholula) was a major Postclassic pilgrimage city-state in the Puebla Valley, dominated by the Tlachihualtepetl pyramid (the largest pyramid by volume in the Americas) and the cult of Quetzalcoatl. After Toltec-Chichimec conquest c. 1200, it became a wealthy market and religious center allied with the Aztec Triple Alliance. In October 1519, Hernán Cortés's forces, accompanied by Tlaxcalan allies and prompted by Doña Marina (Malintzin), carried out the Cholula Massacre — surrounding the city's nobles in the temple courtyard and killing an estimated 3,000–6,000 unarmed Cholulteca (some sources say up to 30,000) in a few hours. The massacre was strategically aimed at terrorizing potential allies of Tenochtitlan and is documented in both Spanish (Cortés, Bernal Díaz) and indigenous (Florentine Codex) accounts. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1400, strategy=capital_near_centroid, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Tollan-Cholollan
name_variants.context: Honorific name asserting succession from Tollan (Tula)

territory_changes.description: Toltec-Chichimec migrants from the north conquer Cholula and re-found it as a Quetzalcoatl pilgrimage center, recorded in the Historia Tolteca-Chichimeca.

Cholula Massacre: Cortés summons Cholula's nobility and warriors into the courtyard of the Quetzalcoatl temple, then orders his troops to kill them. Indigenous accounts in the Florentine Codex describe Spanish soldiers slaughtering unarmed people. Estimates range from 3,000 to 30,000 dead in a few hours; the city is then sacked by Tlaxcalan allies.
Franciscans found a convent at Cholula and begin demolishing temples; the Tlachihualtepetl pyramid is largely buried under colonial construction.

sources.citation: Restall, Matthew. Seven Myths of the Spanish Conquest. Oxford University Press, 2003
Plunket, Patricia, and Gabriela Uruñuela. Holy Places and Sacred Landscapes in Mesoamerican Archaeology: The Great Pyramid of Cholula. University Press of Colorado, 2018

ethical_notes:
The Marajoara culture inhabited Marajó Island at the mouth of the Amazon for nearly a millennium, building large artificial mounds (tesos) up to 10 meters high to elevate villages, fields and cemeteries above the seasonally flooded floodplain. They produced one of the most elaborate ceramic traditions of the pre-Columbian Americas (anthropomorphic burial urns, tanga pubic covers). The 'Marajoara' designation is the modern Brazilian Portuguese label; the indigenous name of the polity is unknown because the people were largely extinct by Portuguese arrival, with descendants likely absorbed into the Aruã and other coastal Amazonian groups who in turn were decimated by 17th–18th century Portuguese slaving and disease. The polygon represents the cultural reach across Marajó and adjacent estuarine islands. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (800, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

[v6.30-displaced-rollback] Boundary reverted to capital-based approximation: aourednik fuzzy match placed polygon centroid=2033km / edge=0km from capital (exceeded thresholds). Regenerated as 300.0km radius circle.

territory_changes.description: Marajoara mound-building tradition begins; Camutins and other teso complexes initiate construction.
Population peaks at an estimated 100,000–500,000 (estimates vary widely). Complex social hierarchy with female-dominated elite burials in painted polychrome urns.

Marajoara mound construction ceases; cause debated (climate fluctuation, internal collapse, disease introduced from coastal contacts). Successor populations (Aruã) occupy a much-reduced cultural landscape.
Portuguese slaving expeditions (descimentos) and missionization of the lower Amazon decimate the surviving Aruã and related successor populations of Marajó.

sources.citation: Roosevelt, Anna C. Moundbuilders of the Amazon: Geophysical Archaeology on Marajo Island, Brazil. Academic Press, 1991
Schaan, Denise P. Cultura Marajoara. Senac, 2010

ethical_notes: The pre-contact ancestors of the Kuikuro and other Upper Xingu peoples (Kalapalo, Matipu, Nahukuá) constructed an integrated network of plaza-towns connected by long, straight roads and surrounded by orchards, fish ponds and managed forests across an area of roughly 20,000 km² in the headwaters of the Xingu River — what archaeologist Michael Heckenberger calls 'galactic clusters' of garden cities. The system was disrupted by the demographic collapse of the 16th–17th centuries, when European-introduced diseases (smallpox, measles, influenza) killed an estimated 90%+ of Amazonian populations, even where direct European contact was rare. The Upper Xingu peoples regrouped at smaller scale; today they share the Xingu Indigenous Park (created 1961) and remain politically active in defense of Amazonian rights. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1300, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

territory_changes.description: Plaza-town network reaches peak development with major settlements 30–50 ha each, linked by engineered roads and bridges; population estimated in the tens of thousands.

Indirect epidemic transmission (downstream from Portuguese settlement on the lower Amazon) collapses Upper Xingu populations by an estimated 90%; large plaza-towns abandoned, survivors regroup in much smaller villages.
First sustained European contact via Karl von den Steinen's expedition; survivors documented as small population of intermarried multi-language communities.
Brazilian government creates Parque Indígena do Xingu (initiated by the Villas-Bôas brothers), the first Indigenous-protected area in Brazil; the Kuikuro and 15 other peoples reside within.

sources.citation: Heckenberger, Michael J. The Ecology of Power: Culture, Place, and Personhood in the Southern Amazon, A.D. 1000–2000. Routledge, 2005
Mann, Charles C. 1491: New Revelations of the Americas Before Columbus. Vintage, 2005

ethical_notes: The Chachapoya ('Cloud Warriors,' from Quechua sacha-puya, 'people of the woods of the cloud forest') were a confederation of independent señoríos in the upper Amazonian flank of the Peruvian Andes, famous for the massive stone fortress of Kuelap, sarcophagus burials at Karajía, and the cliff mausolea of the Laguna de los Cóndores. Their indigenous self-designation in the original Chachapoya language is largely lost; 'Chachapoya' is the Quechua exonym imposed by their Inca conquerors. Inca emperor Túpac Yupanqui conquered them around 1470, deporting populations under the mitma (forced relocation) system; the Chachapoya then largely allied with Pizarro against the Inca, only to be devastated by Spanish epidemics — by 1547 the population had fallen by an estimated 90%, ending Chachapoya political identity.

territory_changes.region: Cloud forest of NE Peru (Amazonas region)
territory_changes.description: Inca Túpac Yupanqui conquers the Chachapoya señoríos after a hard campaign. Many are deported as mitmas (forced colonists) to Cuzco and other parts of the empire.
Chachapoya rebel against Inca rule and ally with Pizarro; their leader Guamán is rewarded with titles by Spanish but quickly subordinated.
Smallpox and other epidemics, exacerbated by encomienda labor demands, reduce the Chachapoya population from an estimated 300,000+ to under 30,000 within two decades of Spanish contact.

sources.citation: Schjellerup, Inge R. Incas and Spaniards in the Conquest of the Chachapoyas. Göteborg University, 1997
Church, Warren B., and Adriana von Hagen. Chachapoyas: Cultural Development at an Andean Cloud Forest Crossroads. In Handbook of South American Archaeology (Springer), 2008

ethical_notes: Xaragua (Jaragua) was one of the five Taíno cacicazgos of Quisqueya/Ayiti (Hispaniola), occupying the southwestern peninsula (modern Haiti's Sud and Grand'Anse departments and the western Dominican Republic). Cacique Bohechío ruled with his sister Anacaona, herself wife of cacique Caonabo of Maguana, who became the most renowned female cacica in Taíno history. In 1503 Spanish governor Nicolás de Ovando invited eighty Xaragua nobles to a ceremonial feast at Yaguana, then ordered them locked into a building and burned alive; Anacaona was hanged in 1504. The killings effectively ended Taíno organized resistance on Hispaniola. The pre-contact Taíno population of the island, estimated at 1–3 million, was reduced to under 200 by mid-16th century — one of the most rapid demographic collapses in human history. Modern Dominican and Puerto Rican populations carry significant Taíno mtDNA. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1400, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Xaraguá
name_variants.context: Taíno spelling preserved in early colonial sources

territory_changes.description: Columbus's first voyage; Bohechío and Anacaona initially receive Spanish envoys peacefully.
Bartolomé Colón forces Bohechío to pay tribute in cotton and provisions; Spanish encomienda system begins to extract Taíno labor.

Yaguana Massacre: Governor Nicolás de Ovando lures eighty Xaragua nobles into a ceremonial reception, then orders the doors barred and the building set ablaze, burning the assembled nobles alive. Spanish troops simultaneously attack other Taíno present.
Cacica Anacaona is hanged by Spanish authorities, ending the Xaragua paramountcy. Survivors are distributed to encomiendas across the island.

Pre-contact Taíno population (estimated 1–3 million across Hispaniola) reduced to under 200 by combined effects of forced labor, smallpox (1518), violence, and starvation. The Spanish import enslaved Africans to replace the destroyed labor force.

sources.citation: Keegan, William F. Taíno Indian Myth and Practice: The Arrival of the Stranger King. University Press of Florida, 2007
Las Casas, Bartolomé de. Brevísima relación de la destrucción de las Indias. 1552 (multiple modern critical editions)
